title: Add Areas of Non-Overlapping Rectangles
description: Find the total area of a composite figure by adding the areas of smaller non-overlapping rectangles.
condition: A composite figure is partitioned into two or more non-overlapping rectangles with known side lengths.
response: Compute each rectangle’s area (length × width) and add the areas.
label: Principle / Model
example: If a figure is split into a $10 \times 4$ rectangle and a $4 \times 4$ rectangle, then total area $= 10 \times 4 + 4 \times 4 = 40 + 16 = 56$.
notes: Applies only when parts do not overlap.

title: Area as Count of Unit Squares
description: Understand that the area of a figure is the number of unit squares that cover it without gaps or overlaps.
condition: Asked to describe or determine the area of a plane figure using unit squares.
response: State that area equals the number of unit squares covering the figure without gaps or overlaps.
label: Concept
example: A figure covered by 11 unit squares has area 11 square units because area is the number of unit squares covering it.

title: Area as Equal Groups of Unit Squares
description: Recognize that the area of a rectangle equals the total number of unit squares arranged in equal rows or columns.
condition: A rectangle is partitioned into equal rows and columns of unit squares.
response: Interpret the total area as rows × columns (or columns × rows) equal groups.
label: Principle / Model
example: A rectangle with 5 columns and 3 rows has area $5 \times 3 = 15$ square units because there are 5 equal groups of 3 squares.
notes: Core conceptual model connecting arrays to multiplication.

title: Area Formula for a Rectangle
description: Compute the area of a rectangle by multiplying its side lengths.
condition: Given side lengths $l$ and $w$ of a rectangle.
label: Principle / Model
example: If a rectangle is 6 units by 5 units, its area is $6 \times 5 = 30$ square units.
notes: Builds on array interpretation of multiplication.

title: Check for Gaps or Overlaps in Tiling
description: Determine whether a set of placed unit squares fully covers a figure without gaps or overlaps.
condition: Given a figure covered by unit squares that may overlap or leave empty spaces.
response: State whether the tiling is valid for measuring area and justify based on presence of gaps or overlaps.
label: Rule / Plan
example: If some squares overlap and some parts of the rectangle are uncovered, then the arrangement cannot be used to determine the area.
notes: Requires explicit reasoning about full coverage.

title: Commutative Property of Multiplication
description: Recognize that reversing the order of factors does not change the product.
label: Concept
notes: Explains why both 6×5 and 5×6 represent the same area.

title: Count Unit Squares for Area
description: Determine area by counting all unit squares that cover a figure.
condition: A figure is drawn on a square grid with visible unit squares.
response: Count all full unit squares to determine total area.
label: Production / Skill
example: If a shape covers 13 full grid squares, its area is 13 square units.
notes: May involve systematic tracking (e.g., numbering squares).

title: Decompose Rectilinear Figure into Rectangles
description: Split a composite rectilinear figure into two or more non-overlapping rectangles to simplify area calculation.
condition: Given a rectilinear figure composed of horizontal and vertical sides.
response: Partition the figure into non-overlapping rectangles whose side lengths are known or can be determined.
label: Production / Skill
example: Split an L-shaped figure into an $8 \times 7$ rectangle and a $5 \times 2$ rectangle before finding total area.

title: Distributive Property of Area
description: Represent the area of a rectangle as a sum of products by decomposing one factor.
condition: A rectangle with side lengths where one side can be decomposed into a sum (e.g., $7 = 5 + 2$).
response: Express area as $a \times (b + c) = a \times b + a \times c$.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Connects area model to distributive property.

title: Express Area in Square Units
description: State the numerical area together with the correct unit label 'square units.'
condition: After determining the number of unit squares covering a figure.
response: Report the measure as a number followed by 'square units.'
label: Concept
example: If the count is 20, report: 20 square units.

title: Find Factor Pair for Given Area
description: Determine possible side lengths of a rectangle given its area by identifying factor pairs.
condition: A rectangle’s area is given as a whole number.
response: List pairs of whole numbers whose product equals the given area.
label: Principle / Model
example: If area is $12$, possible side lengths include $(1,12)$, $(2,6)$, and $(3,4)$.

title: Generate Side Lengths Under Constraints
description: Find pairs of side lengths that satisfy both an area requirement and side-length constraints.
condition: Given constraints such as area ≥ target value and each side length < given limit.
response: Produce side-length pairs whose product meets the area requirement and individually satisfy the limits.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Involves coordinating multiplication with inequality constraints.

title: Interpret 'At Least' as ≥
description: Translate the phrase 'at least' into a greater-than-or-equal-to inequality.
condition: A quantity is described as 'at least' a given number.
response: Represent or apply the condition as ≥ that number.
label: Fact

title: Interpret 'Less Than' as <
description: Translate the phrase 'less than' into a strict inequality.
condition: A side length is described as 'less than' a given number.
response: Represent or apply the condition as < that number.
label: Fact
example: 'Each side less than 8 feet' means $l < 8$ and $w < 8$.

title: Match Expression to Rectangular Area
description: Determine whether a numerical expression represents the area of a given rectangle.
condition: Given a rectangle’s side lengths and a candidate numerical expression.
response: Check whether the expression equals length × width of the rectangle.
label: Principle / Model
example: For a $6$ by $5$ rectangle, test $6 \times 5 = 30$ (match) but $6 + 5 + 6 + 5 = 22$ (does not match area).
notes: Requires distinguishing multiplication (area) from addition (perimeter or partial counts).

title: Subtract Area of Missing Rectangle
description: Find the area of a composite figure by subtracting the area of a removed rectangle from a larger enclosing rectangle.
condition: A composite figure can be viewed as a large rectangle with a smaller rectangular region removed.
response: Compute area of large rectangle, compute area of removed rectangle, subtract.
label: Principle / Model
example: If a $10 \times 8$ rectangle has a $6 \times 4$ rectangle removed, area $= 10 \times 8 - 6 \times 4 = 80 - 24 = 56$.

title: Use Opposite Sides Equal in Rectangle
description: Use the property that opposite sides of a rectangle are equal to determine unknown side lengths.
condition: A rectangle has one side length unknown but the opposite side length is given.
response: Set the unknown side equal to the known opposite side length.
label: Concept
example: If the top side is $9$ ft, then the bottom side is also $9$ ft.
notes: Supports finding missing dimensions before area calculation.
